reasoning: The $template_id variable in tab_design.php line 35 is the iteration key of the array returned by WPCaptcha_Functions::get_templates(). That function (libs/functions.php lines 1082-1315) is a fully hardcoded static method that constructs and returns a PHP array with literal string keys: 'white', 'orange' (implied), 'red', 'green', 'blue', 'gray'. No apply_filters() hook wraps the return value, no database read populates the keys, and no user-controlled input touches the array at any point. The install_template() handler (line 1324) applies sanitize_key() to $_GET['template'] and validates it with array_key_exists() against the same hardcoded array before persisting — so no attacker-controlled string ever becomes a stored template key. The page is also admin-only (requires manage_options capability). While $template_id is concatenated without esc_attr() into a class attribute and without esc_url() wrapping in the href, the source is compile-time constants making this an unexploitable code-quality issue. No attacker-controlled data reaches the sink in the current codebase. Classified as false positive / latent theoretical risk only.
